coach_notes.engagement_notes: Prefers claude.ai chat pacing for live exercise sessions — CC felt slower than chat (2026-04-30 feedback). For Step 6 persistence, always show human-readable bullets (not raw JSON) before writing. PV training ladder: recognition (Smart-mode quiz filtered to PV) → particle_sort → transform/free_write. Don't start cold on PV transform without a recognition warm-up in the same session or the day before. Two-part format (8-10 particle_sort + 4-5 transform same session) validated 2026-05-02 — combined 73-96%, recognition→production within single session works. Use this format for future PV sessions. Prefers concise explanations; pushes back on verbose responses. In two-stage sessions, use **different contexts/sentences in recognition vs production** — same items, different stems, to avoid carry-over that masks true production gaps. DEFAULT EXERCISE FORMAT (confirmed 2026-05-11, strong positive feedback — 'I like it very much, let's do all my exercises as conversations'): run ALL exercise types **conversation-style** — present one item at a time, he answers, I recast/nudge, then the next — NOT as an upfront numbered list. The numbered-list format reads as boring to him; conversational pacing keeps engagement high and tends to surface useful clarifying questions (e.g. asked about biz_oil register tiers mid-session 2026-05-11; 'belt and braces?', 'is fault uncountable here?' during the 2026-05-11 emphasis session). Keep batches modest (~8-10 items) regardless of type. Applies to particle_sort, transform, error_correction, article_drill, translation, phrase_swap_drill (CC mirror-recast) — and free_write was already conversational. EMPHASIS LADDER (added 2026-05-11 after the inversion breakthrough — 5/6 on the historically-0/5 fronting+inversion cluster WITH the trigger-word list scaffolded): before any cold emphasis transform, run a ~2-min recognition warm-up on the negative/restrictive trigger words (spot/pick the correctly-inverted form) — mirrors the PV recognition→production ladder. The gap is cold recall of the trigger list + the inversion reflex, not competence. Don't treat C1 emphasis as an abandoned ceiling; it's tractable with spaced reps. Within an emphasis session go simple→complex: it-clefts → wh-clefts → fronting+inversion → emphatic do; explain the machinery on every item and recap on misses (this pacing was explicitly requested and worked 2026-05-11).
strong_patterns: PV preposition pairings mastered 2026-05-02 (6/6 error_correction drill after two-stage session isolation)
weak_patterns: article 'the' overuse with abstract / zero-article nouns (at the stage, take the action)

phrasal verb production — 'get across' cold-production gap (recognition with options clean, cold transform fails: produced 'get about' 2026-05-03). 'get around to' stickiest particle, 3rd consecutive session in recognition; produces cleanly after one same-session exposure. Latinate-causal PV separability with long NPs (bring about, give rise to) — tendency to separate where adjacent is natural.

opposite/contrast preposition: 'opposite of' (not 'opposite to') for nouns; 'unlike / in contrast to / as opposed to' for clauses. Recurred 2x in 2026-05-03 free_write session. Candidate for focused 5-6 item error_correction drill (per the PV preposition drill model from 2026-05-02).

comparison 'than' vs 'that': Russian-L1 trap (no English homophone for чем). Recurred 2x in 2026-05-04 free_write session ('way faster that state companies'; 'more restrictive that I thought'). Candidate for focused 5-6 item error_correction drill per opposite/of drill model 2026-05-04.

PV-adjacent substitution at C1-business register: produces a real PV in the right semantic direction but loses the specific collocation force the source carries. Surfaced 5x in single 2026-05-10 session: bring_up for get_across (mention vs convey-with-landing); end_up for turn_out (final-state vs revealed-after-the-fact); get_to for bring_about (reach vs cause); brought_down for cut_down_on (lower-an-abstract vs habit-cutback); get_into for go_about (enter-state vs methodical-approach). Distinct from the existing 'cold-production gap' note — this is the *mechanism* of the gap. Recommendation: pre-submission self-check 'does this PV carry the SPECIFIC collocation, or just the direction?'
I need the explanation to read and select options → I need it readable enough that I can scan and pick [claude_collab]

Hedge variety — Russian 'может быть / наверное / пожалуй' all map to 'maybe'; defaults to 'maybe' as the only sentence-opener hedge. Vary with 'perhaps', 'I wonder if', 'what if we', 'could we', 'possibly', 'I think'. Candidate for focused 5-6 item error_correction drill (per the opposite/of model 2026-05-04).

Russian SVO carryover with dummy 'it' — defaults to 'It was a [group of X]' where English prefers concrete subject + verb ('They were a [group of X]' / subject-first). Surfaced in 2026-05-11 external review ('it was a bunch of technical persons'). Candidate for focused 5-6 item error_correction drill on dummy-it restructuring.

'It worth to X' — drops 'is' on existential 'it's' AND uses to-infinitive after 'worth' (should be gerund). Two-step fix: 'it worth to X' → 'it's worth X-ing'. Russian-L1 trap (no English homophone for 'стоит'). Candidate for short error_correction drill.

Intensifier overreliance — defaults to 'definitely' across consecutive sentences. Vary with 'clearly / absolutely / certainly', or omit ('it's not a managerial position' is already strong). Candidate for register-marked rewriting drill.

gerund after suggest/start/help/recommend/avoid — defaults to 'to V' form. 4-month evidence across claude.ai chats: 'I started to have my doubts' (2026-02 + 2026-05), 'I suggest to draw out' (2026-04), 'I suggest to keep' (2026-05), 'Helped to avert' (2026-04). Cross-context [biz_oil] + [claude_collab]. Candidate for focused 8-10 item error_correction drill on the closed verb set (suggest/start/help/recommend/avoid/finish/keep/consider + V-ing).

'in case of X' calque (в случае X) — defaults to 'in case of' where native uses 'for X' / 'when X' / 'if X'. Recurred 2026-02 ('in case of large modeling exercises' [biz_oil]) and 2026-04 ('in case of shorter periods' [leisure_sport]). Cross-context, easy drill: 4-6 item error_correction on the conditional substitutes.
forget to say that → I forgot to mention [claude_collab]

recent_observations.note: [data] Emphasis session — secondary findings (cleft handling, not inversion): (1) it-cleft TAIL over-editing — rephrases/rearranges the relative-clause leftover instead of just lifting the spotlighted element out: #1 'the missed deadline by the contractor' (awkward agent-by on a noun), #2 slid 'only' to the pre-verb slot ('only approved' = merely-approved reading) — needs a 'lift, don't remodel; leave the tail's internal order alone' rule + adverb-scope awareness. (2) After 'What we should do is...' he uses 'is TO fix' — should be bare 'is fix'. (3) Tense vigilance: drifts present<->past when recasting (#3 'worried' for 'is worried'; #5 'frustrates' for 'frustrated') and over-applies past perfect (#10). Candidate focused micro-drill: it-cleft 'lift not remodel' + tense-faithfulness when transforming. Engagement: full 30 min conversational, no fatigue, asked good clarifying Qs ('belt and braces?', 'is fault uncountable here?') — conversational format validated for hard grammar, not just lexical/PV work.

Good to go, Artem.

Egor spent ___ year preparing for IELTS before his first attempt.
A

Correct. First-mention countable singular → "a year".

---

Nicole's K-pop playlist is ___ music she listens to on the school bus every morning.

Not quite. "The music she listens to" — the post-modifier ("she listens to on the school bus") identifies exactly which music, so it needs **the**.

---

Anna booked a padel court, but ___ court was already taken by another group.
The

Correct. Second-mention → "the court".

---

Ernest plays ___ football with his friends after school on Fridays.

Correct. Generic sport/activity → zero article.

---

Artem had to fly to ___ Bahrain for an urgent client meeting last month.

Correct. Country names take zero article.

---

The vendor sent ___ proposal that completely missed the project scope.
The

Not quite. "A proposal" — it's first mention, no prior reference in the sentence, so it needs **a**. "The" would work only if the listener already knew which proposal.

---

Anna hurt her shoulder and spent two days in ___ hospital.

Correct. Fixed expression "in hospital" (BrE) → zero article.

---

Artem's cycling coach suggested ___ training plan focused on threshold intervals.
A

Correct. First-mention countable singular → "a training plan".

---

___ progress Egor made in his writing module surprised his IELTS tutor.

Not quite. "The progress Egor made" — the post-modifier ("Egor made in his writing module") identifies exactly which progress, so it needs **the**.

---

Nicole's school puts ___ emphasis on academic performance over extracurriculars.

Correct. Zero article — abstract uncountable noun used generically here.

---

That's 10/10 items done. **Score: 8/10.**

Misses:
- **Item 6** — "a proposal" (first-mention, not "the")
- **Item 9** — "the progress" (post-modifier makes it identified)

Both misses are the same pattern: post-modifier identification. Worth flagging for next session.
Good

Good session. The post-modifier trap ("the [noun] that/Egor made/she listens to") is your one consistent gap here — your instinct is to drop the article when you see an abstract or uncountable noun, but a post-modifier overrides that and forces **the**. Keep that rule handy for next time.
That's it — wrap up the drill.

**Saved.**

messages.content: Please explain in more depth (after wrong submission: I'm looking for your last week's email)

Good attempt, Artem — let me unpack this carefully.

What you got right: you correctly used the present continuous ("I'm looking"), and you correctly chose the phrasal verb **look for** (to search for something). Those are the two main grammar/vocabulary points of the sentence, so well done there.

What needs adjusting is the noun phrase **"your last week's email"**. The issue isn't grammar exactly — it's that you stacked two possessive-style modifiers onto one noun:

- **your** (possessive determiner)
- **last week's** (possessive 's)

In English, you can normally only have one determiner slot before a noun. So "your email" ✅ and "last week's email" ✅ both work alone, but combining them sounds awkward and is generally avoided. Native speakers solve this by converting one of them into a prepositional phrase placed *after* the noun:

➡️ **"your email from last week"**
(your = before the noun; from last week = after the noun)

This is the natural pattern: **[determiner] + noun + [from + time expression]**.

Another example with the same structure:
- ❌ "I can't find your yesterday's message."
- ✅ "I can't find your message from yesterday." — *Я не могу найти твоё сообщение со вчерашнего дня.*

The same logic applies with other determiners too: "the photo from our trip," "his speech from Monday," "her text from this morning." Whenever you already have *my/your/his/her/the/this*, push the time reference to after the noun with **from**.

A small side note: native speakers do sometimes say "last week's email" when there's no other possessor — e.g., "Did you read last week's email?" That's fine because "last week's" is the only determiner. The clash only happens when you try to add **your** on top.

**Takeaway:** When you already have a possessive like *your*, move the time reference after the noun using *from* — "your email from last week," not "your last week's email."

messages.content: Hey Artem — ready when you are. What's on your mind today? Options: work week, F1 (Miami coming up), bike (got out at all over the weekend?).
bike. just finished an outdoor ride
Nice. How's the heat? May in Bahrain is right on the edge of rideable outdoors. Tell me about the ride — distance, pace, how you felt at the end.
Today was hotter than usual. A lack of wind really heated up my ride. Two days ago was very windy, and I don't have a problem to ride up to 11 am

Two fixes: 'have a problem to ride' → 'have a problem riding' (gerund after have a problem). 'up to 11 am' → 'until 11 am' (until for time endpoints; up to is for limits/quantities). 'A lack of wind heated up my ride' is understandable but slightly off — 'With no wind, the ride felt much hotter' is more natural. ✓ 'A lack of wind' clean indefinite article.
расскажи побольше я часто вижу исправления until till to

[Detailed explanation: until=till (interchangeable, till informal); by=deadline (no later than); to=clock fragments / X-to-Y ranges; up to=quantities. Russian до maps differently. Mini drill: 1) 'send numbers to Thursday' 2) 'I'll be at office till 6' 3) 'wait up to 30 minutes'.]
1 by, 2 until, 3 to is OK. why till is wrong in 2? you said until and till is same

Sharp pushback — partly right. Item 2 was a trap, already correct (till=until both work). Your 'until' also fine, just don't think 'till' was wrong. Item 3 'up to 30 minutes' correct because 30 minutes = quantity/duration (max amount), different from clock-time '11 am' which needs until.
back to bike
When still and hot, how do you cope? How long was today's ride and where?
I tried to ride slower. Anyway I rode today with a bunch of guys from work. They are usually pretty slow. On the way back I pedaled hard, despite the heat I wanted to get home as early as possible.

Main correction: 'as early as possible' → 'as soon as possible' / 'as quickly as possible' (early=time of day; soon=minimal delay; Russian как можно раньше maps to as soon as possible here). Comma after 'despite the heat'. ✓ 'a bunch of guys from work', 'despite the heat' (no 'of'), 'on the way back'.
Come on. Two weak guys rode a shorter loop. We rode fast after they got to their cars.
Misread the structure — got it. ✓ Clean turn. Optional upgrade: 'two weak guys' → 'two slower lads', 'tapped out / peeled off / called it early'. 'Weak' in English is harsher than RU 'слабые'.
35 km/h on the way back, which is about 3-4 km/h faster than my solo rides. It was just the two of us, of course, we took turns. He's faster since I'm still acclimatizing from much chiller Turkey.

'much chiller Turkey' → 'chillier' (chill is noun/verb/slang, chilly is the temperature adjective). 'acclimatizing from' → 'acclimatizing TO the heat after Turkey' (you acclimatize to a climate, not from). RU привыкаю к жаре. ✓ 'we took turns' perfect cycling idiom.
chiller was autocorrect, not me.
Strike that. The acclimatize to/after preposition note still stands. Where were you in Turkey, when did you get back?

We spent a month in Turkey, when local tensions with Iran resulted in constant drone and missile attacks across the Gulf. I met my old friends there and really enjoyed a completely different scenery and nice but rainy weather. Totally opposite to Bahrain.

'a completely different scenery' → drop 'a' (scenery is uncountable, like weather/traffic/advice). Interesting flip from your usual 'the'-overuse — here you added 'a' to an uncountable. 'I met my old friends' → 'caught up with / met up with old friends' (met alone often = first-time meeting). 'Totally opposite to Bahrain' → 'the opposite OF Bahrain'. ✓ 'constant drone and missile attacks across the Gulf' clean. ✓ 'nice but rainy weather' — uncountable weather handled correctly with zero article.
We were for 2 weeks in Fethiye enjoying its small and vibrant town with a large cycling community of Russian expats.

Two RU L1 word-order traps: 'We were for 2 weeks in Fethiye' → 'We were in Fethiye for 2 weeks' (place before duration in English). 'enjoying its small and vibrant town' — Fethiye IS the town, doesn't have one. Use apposition: '...in Fethiye — a small, vibrant town with...' Style: 'a small, vibrant town' (comma) > 'small and vibrant town' (and).

Oh, yes, Fethiye is widely known for best gravel routes in the world. A lot of Russians are there due to a nice combination of these gravel opportunities, good weather and easy connection to major Russian cities. The next day I arrived I blended in into the group of fellow riders and went for a long 7-hour rides across the coast.

Hit two weak patterns hard. 'known for best gravel routes' → 'known for THE best gravel routes' (superlatives need the — fossilised articles, omission direction this time). 'blended in into the group' → 'blended in WITH the group' or 'blended INTO the group' (PV preposition pairing — same family as give up on / take over from). 'The next day I arrived' → 'The day after I arrived'. Quick: 'a long 7-hour rides' → singular 'ride' (with 'a'); 'across the coast' → 'along the coast' (across=perpendicular). ✓ 'widely known for', 'fellow riders', 'due to a nice combination'.
That was unusually long for me. Especially after Bahrain. I have to recover myself for a few days and then start to slowly rebuild my fitness riding solo on flatter terrain.

RU L1 reflexive trap: 'recover myself' → 'recover' (recover is intransitive in English; RU восстанавливаться is reflexive). Tense drift: narrating past, slipped to present — 'I had to recover... and then started to slowly rebuild'. ✓ 'unusually long for me', 'rebuild my fitness', 'flatter terrain'.
I'm happy to do more
Where was the other half of the Turkey trip? How did the family handle a month away from Bahrain?

Then I moved to Alanya. I have a scheduled cycling camp there with my old coach from Moscow. Alanya is much bigger touristic town compared to Fethiye. The family didn't like it much due to crowds and dense building in the opposite to more relaxed Fethiye. But I was busy pedaling hard into the mountains.

'much bigger touristic town' → 'a much more touristy town' (article missing; touristic is RU/FR calque; native: tourist/touristy). ⚠️ 'in the opposite to Fethiye' → SECOND occurrence this session of opposite/contrast preposition error (earlier 'opposite to Bahrain'). Fix: 'unlike / in contrast to / as opposed to' Fethiye. Worth a focused drill. 'dense building' → 'dense buildings / overdevelopment'. Quick: 'moved to Alanya' → 'went/headed' (move=relocate); 'I have a scheduled' → 'I had a cycling camp scheduled'. ✓ 'pedaling hard into the mountains'.
It was a build program when you spend a lot of time climbing. Of course, we have the rest days with only 2 hours of cruising along the coast by highway. But most of our time we spend climbing.

'the rest days' → 'rest days' (zero article — your fossilised the-overuse pattern with generic plural categories; rest days = generic concept here, not specific known set). Tense: 'we have... we spend' → 'we had... we spent' (narrative past). 'build program when you spend' → 'where you spend / that involves' (when=time, where=describing thing). 'by highway' → 'along the coastal highway / on the highway'. 'most of our time we spend climbing' → 'we spent most of our time climbing'. ✓✓ 'along the coast' — corrected from earlier 'across the coast' slip. Same-session uptake.

messages.content: Suggested topics: Monday post-trip, rig schedule, F1 Miami.
first one
How's the inbox after a week off?

We come back to Bahrain almost three weeks ago. Immediately we noticed lower traffic since many families left Bahrain during the bombings. Everyone in the office was happy to return after a month of remote work.
Fix: came back / got back (past). Stiff: 'Immediately we noticed' → 'first thing we noticed'. Asked about the bombings.
Yes, you forgot to check the news. Trump and Israel started a war with Iran in the last day of March. And Iran retaliated with bombing of all nearby Gulf states harboring US military bases.
Fix: on the last day of March (not in); retaliate by V-ing (not with bombing of).

yes, only bases and industries affiliated with USA were hit. Everything was pretty far away from our villa, but it's a tiny island, and we can heard sirens and SAM explosions almost every night and day. Therefore, we decided to spend time somewhere else and travelled to Turkey for a month.
Fix: the US (article with country); could hear (modal+base); register: Therefore→So, take off for a bit.

My kids and wife were shocked. Of course, the risk for us was low, but the sitation was pretty depressing. Nowadays everyone hopes that it was a one-off thing, but many people has decided to leave the region for good.
Fix: situation typo; many people have. ✓ a one-off thing, leave for good — idiomatic.
Mostly expats families, but not necessary Western. Now we are also searching for a school in Almaty to move back, and apparently many places have been already taken by returned Kazakhstani citizens.
Fix: expat families (noun-as-modifier singular); not necessarily; have already been taken (adverb placement in perfect).

My contract with Bapco is going to end soon. In the current situation when the refinery is bombed and idling, they don't have money to extend most of expat contracts. As a result, we are exploring Plan B - moving back to Kazakhstan where my mother and eldest son live.
Fix: the refinery's been bombed and is sitting idle (perfect passive + collocation); most expat contracts (or most of the); register: contract is winding down.

My contract run out in the end of June right after school ends and we are good to go. I'm still looking for a job in the Gulf. Still have enough time to get a place in a private company which are way faster that a state companies like Bapco. But economic situation is tough. Many companies reduce hiring, and summer is kicking in leading to lower business activity. Ideally I need to wait till autumn. Many company will realize which vacancies to fill and restart hiring actively. But we need to decide where the kids will go to school next year.
Fix: contract runs out at the end of June; than (not that); the economic situation; Many companies. ✓ kicking in (PV unprompted), good to go (idiom).
I had several good interview rounds before everything happened. Now I'm ramping up my efforts and asking my connections for help.
✓ ramping up (uptake). Nudge: reaching out to my contacts.
yes, everything I worked on was stopped. O&G Downstream is ideal, but the sector is dominated by state companies - will take too long. Mining and consulting sound good for me too.
Fix: sound good to me (preposition L1 calque). Recast: got put on hold.
It's very difficult to get back to consulting. Different vibe requiring more energy compared to state companies. I have to persuade hard that I can manage.
✓ get back to (PV unprompted). Fix: persuade hard → make a strong case / push to convince. Asked want vs needs-must.

I want more energetic company for sure. I'm very unhappy right now in state co. I promised myself to avoid anything state related after leaving Rosneft, and I regret my decision to join Bapco. It's a good experience but very dissappointing in the end.
Fix: a more energetic company (article); has been (present perfect, looking back); typo disappointing. ✓ in the end correct, contrasting at the end of June from earlier.

the whole current situation is a decisive factor. We have to move again. I knew that Bapco is temporary, but expect to land a new job easily. It appears that the Gulf job market is much more restrictive that I thought. Everyone looks for a perfect match. My CV is too chaotic for most companies here.
Fix: more restrictive THAN (recurring 2x); I knew Bapco was temporary, but I expected (backshift + missing subject I). ✓ land a new job. Asked CV framing.
Question: but Bapco is still happening. Could I say 'I knew Bapco was'. knew was in the past before joining Bapco. and again I still work for Bapco
Honest answer: both grammatical. Backshift is optional when reported fact still true. 'I knew Bapco is temporary' is fine. Was still smoother in extended past narrative.
want to go for lunch. let's wrap up

messages.content: ready

Hey Artem, let's go! 🎯

**Cue 1:**
Ты с Крисом договорились насчёт встречи на следующей неделе — как бы ты сказал это коллеге?
Chris, let's meet next week
Almost there — the cue is about telling a *colleague* that you and Chris have already reached an agreement. Try again with that in mind: who needs to be in the sentence?
Chris and I agreed to meet next week

Nice! "Chris and I agreed" is exactly the natural move here.

The stiff version to avoid: "we agreed" — technically fine, but in work emails/messages it's often unclear *who* "we" refers to. Naming both parties ("CC and I agreed") removes ambiguity instantly.

---

**Cue 2:**
Ты рассказываешь другу про свой лучший результат на велосипеде — тот, что был четыре года назад. Как скажешь по-английски?
I got my best result 4 years ago

Good — that works! The more natural, casual version you'd hear from a cyclist: **"my best from 4 years ago"** or **"what I hit 4 years ago"**.

"My achievement of 4 years ago" is the stiff alternative — it sounds a bit formal/written for casual sport talk.

---

**Cue 3:**
Ты объясняешь коллеге, почему какой-то инструмент или процесс тебе не подходит. Как скажешь кратко по-английски?
It doesn't fit

That works as a general phrase! The more natural, idiomatic version in a work context: **"it isn't relevant for me"** — it signals the thing exists but simply doesn't apply to your situation.

"Is not relevant for my context" is the stiff alternative — "for my context" feels over-formal in casual office talk; "for me" does the job cleanly.

---

**Cue 4:**
Ты на падел-корте, рассказываешь приятелю как вышел на улицу этим утром. Как скажешь?
I leave my villa this morning

Good instinct on the vocab! The natural phrasing: **"I left the villa this morning"** (or "stepped outside").

"Go out of my villa" is the stiff version — "leave the villa" or "step outside" is how expats would naturally say it in casual conversation.

(Also just a frequency note: "left" rather than "leave" since it's this morning — past tense fits here.)

---

**Cue 5:**
Ты на совещании, коллега спрашивает, откуда взялись данные в отчёте. Ты их придумал. Как признаешься? 😄
I made them up

Perfect — "made them up" is exactly the natural form! 🎯

The stiff alternative: "invented them" — grammatically fine, but in spoken office banter "made up" is far more natural and idiomatic for fabricating data (even as a joke).

---

**Cue 6:**
Ты объясняешь другу, где проходят твои велогонки — онлайн, в приложении Zwift. Как скажешь?
I ride on Zwift

Close — "ride on Zwift" is natural! The cue was specifically about **racing** though, so the target was: **"I race on Zwift"**.

The stiff alternative: "race *in* Zwift" — "on" is the natural preposition for platforms and apps (on Zwift, on Strava, on Instagram). "In" sounds like you're physically inside it.

---

That's all 6! Good session, Artem — the "CC and I agreed" and "made them up" were especially clean. The ones to keep revisiting: **on Zwift** (preposition) and **leave the villa** (natural verb choice). 💪
That's it — wrap up the drill.

**Saved.**

| Mastered today | — |
| Up next | 6 phrases active, 0 retest in ~3 weeks |

That's a phrasal verb meaning "to result/prove to be" — not quite the target here.

The gloss **случаться / происходить** = **occur**. Watch the spelling: one *c*, double *r* → **occur**. The related noun *occurrence* is a classic doubled-letter trap.

Accumulate

✓ Correct.

Wednesday

✓ Correct.

**рекомендовать** — to suggest something as a good option
Recommend

✓ Correct. One *c*, double *m* — a common trap there.

Self-awareness

✓ Correct. Hyphen in the right place too.
